title: Ear tagging sheep
subtitle: How to tag lambs and adult sheep to meet UK identification requirements.
excerpt: All sheep in the UK must be individually identified before they leave their birth holding. The rules differ by birth year and whether the animal moves between holdings. Getting tagging right from birth avoids significant compliance problems later.
sourceNotes: DEFRA, Sheep and goat identification and movement rules (2023). The Sheep and Goats (Records, Identification and Movement) (England) Order 2009 and amendments.

glossaryTerms.definition: County Parish Holding number, a nine-digit code identifying your land holding, issued by the Rural Payments Agency. Required for keeping sheep, goats, cattle, pigs, and deer. Your CPH must appear on your flock tags.
A unique alphanumeric code assigned to your sheep flock by the Rural Payments Agency. Appears on the tags applied to your lambs. Enables individual identification and traceability across movements.

content.text: UK sheep identification law requires that all lambs born on your holding are tagged before they leave that holding, or by 9 months of age if they remain on the birth holding. You need a registered
before you can order tags. Contact the Rural Payments Agency to register if you have not already done so.

Lambs born on your holding and not moving off before 12 months need only a single conventional ear tag in either ear. Lambs that will be moved off the holding before 12 months need two tags: an EID (electronic) tag in the left ear and a conventional visual tag, or two conventional tags. Tags must display your flock mark and an individual number. Order from an approved tag supplier; tags are specific to your flock mark and cannot be bought as generic blanks.

Restrain the lamb against your body or between your knees. Load the tag into the applicator: the stud (pin) goes into the male jaw, the tag body into the female jaw. Position the applicator so the tag will pass through the middle third of the ear, away from the edge (which tears) and away from the thick cartilage ridge at the top. The tag should sit in a relatively flat area of the ear. Apply firm, steady pressure; a hesitant application causes more tearing. Release immediately.

Check the tag is seated correctly and the pin has passed cleanly through the ear. A small amount of blood at the tag site is normal. Monitor the ear over the following 48 hours for swelling or infection. Record the tag number in your holding register on the day of tagging; the register is a legal requirement and must be kept for at least three years.

If a tag is lost, replace it with a replacement tag carrying the same number, marked with an R. Keep a record of the replacement in your holding register. Do not reuse tag numbers. If you cannot identify the animal from the record (for example it was purchased and the seller's records are incomplete), contact the RPA for guidance before applying an incorrect or guessed number.
